title: Amos 4
subtitle: "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — Cows of Bashan and Empty Worship

heb.paragraph: The metaphor addresses the elite women of Samaria as 'cows of Bashan,' the region east of the Sea of Galilee known for its well-fed cattle (cf. Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12). The image is one of pampered indulgence: these women oppress the poor and demand that their husbands supply their luxury. The comparison is deliberately provocative.

refs.note: Isaiah's parallel condemnation of worship without justice: 'I have no pleasure in the blood of bulls and lambs.'
'Strong bulls of Bashan encircle me' — the same Bashan cattle imagery used in a context of threatening power.

notes.note: The 'cows of Bashan' are the wealthy women of Samaria who oppress the poor and crush the needy by demanding luxury from their husbands. Their indulgence was funded by injustice. Amos does not spare anyone from accountability.

God swears by his holiness that these women will be taken away with hooks — a reference to the Assyrian practice of leading captives with hooks in their lips or noses. The luxury of Samaria will end in the humiliation of exile.

Amos sarcastically invites Israel to worship at Bethel and Gilgal — not because their worship pleases God, but because it pleases them. Worship divorced from justice and obedience is nothing but religious self-gratification.

Calvin applies the 'cows of Bashan' critique broadly: anyone who lives in luxury while the poor suffer is complicit in oppression, regardless of gender. The demand for comfort that drives exploitation is the deeper sin.

Calvin reads the sarcastic invitation to worship as a devastating exposure of religious hypocrisy. Israel's multiplied sacrifices are not piety but performance. God is not honored by ritual that lacks repentance.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The address 'cows of Bashan' is unique in prophetic literature. Bashan (the modern Golan Heights) was famous for its lush pastureland and well-fed livestock (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12; Ezek 39:18). The metaphor emphasizes pampered indulgence.

The command 'Go to Bethel and sin' (boʾu vet-el uphishaʿu) uses the imperative sarcastically, a rhetorical device found elsewhere in prophetic speech (cf. Isa 6:9). The verb pashaʿ ('to rebel/transgress') redefines Israel's worship as rebellion.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart emphasizes that the 'cows of Bashan' metaphor targets a specific social class, not women in general. These are the wives of the ruling elite whose demand for luxury creates economic pressure that their husbands pass downward to the poor. The oath 'by his holiness' (v. 2) is the most solemn oath formula in the OT, indicating the certainty of the coming judgment.

Stuart reads the sarcastic worship invitation as Amos' most withering critique of the northern cult. Every detail — sacrifices every morning, tithes every three days, leavened thanksgiving offerings — is exaggerated beyond Torah requirements. The worship is technically enthusiastic but ethically bankrupt.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman argue that the 'cows of Bashan' passage is linked to the sarcastic worship pericope (vv. 4-5) as a single rhetorical unit: the women demand luxury (v. 1), and the cult at Bethel provides the religious cover for the economic system that produces it. Worship and exploitation are structurally connected.

hist.context: This section opens the second 'Hear this word' speech (4:1). The 'cows of Bashan' metaphor targets the women of Samaria's elite, who drive the cycle of exploitation by demanding luxury from their husbands. The passage then turns to Bethel and Gilgal, the northern sanctuaries, where Amos sarcastically invites Israel to multiply their sacrifices and offerings — because they 'love to do this.' The irony is biting: their worship is self-serving performance, not genuine devotion.

heb.gloss: yet you have not returned to me
heb.paragraph: This refrain occurs five times (vv. 6, 8, 9, 10, 11), functioning as both accusation and lament. Each divine plague — famine, drought, blight, pestilence, overthrow — was designed to prompt repentance. The fivefold repetition of Israel's refusal creates a devastating picture of hardened impenitence.

The chapter climaxes with 'Prepare to meet your God, O Israel' — not an evangelistic invitation but a summons to face divine judgment. The God of creation (who forms mountains and creates wind) is coming in person, and Israel must stand before him.

refs.note: The covenant curses of drought, blight, and mildew that Amos' plague recital echoes.
The overthrow of Sodom, explicitly cited in v. 11 as a precedent for divine destruction.
The Revelation parallel: even after plagues, the survivors 'did not repent' — the same pattern of hardened impenitence.

notes.note: Five divine judgments are listed: famine (v. 6), drought (vv. 7-8), crop disease (v. 9), plague and war (v. 10), and catastrophic overthrow (v. 11). Each was God's gracious warning before final judgment. The refrain 'yet you have not returned to me' reveals God's heart: he disciplines in order to restore.

'Prepare to meet your God, O Israel' is not a gospel invitation but a courtroom summons. Israel is being called to face their judge. Since disciplinary warnings have failed, the final confrontation is imminent.

The doxology identifies God as the creator of mountains, wind, and the dawn — the one who 'treads on the heights of the earth.' This is cosmic language, not merely national. The God Israel must face is the Lord of all creation.

Calvin reads the five plagues as graduated discipline: God increases the severity of punishment when lighter corrections are ignored. This is the pattern of divine pedagogy — merciful in intention, escalating in intensity, until the impenitent exhaust even God's patience.

Calvin identifies the doxology as the theological climax: the God of judgment is also the God of creation. There is no escape from one who made the mountains, knows human thoughts, and turns dawn to darkness.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'cleanness of teeth' (niqyon shinnayim) is an idiom for famine: teeth are clean because there is nothing to eat. The metaphor is grimly precise.

The doxology is the first of three in Amos (4:13; 5:8-9; 9:5-6), each celebrating YHWH's cosmic power. These may have been drawn from a hymn used in the temple liturgy, recontextualized by Amos as a reminder of the God Israel faces.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart identifies the five plagues as an intentional reversal of the Exodus plagues: where God struck Egypt to liberate Israel, now he strikes Israel to provoke repentance. The five categories (famine, drought, blight, pestilence, overthrow) correspond to Deuteronomy 28 covenant curses, confirming that Amos reads Israel's suffering through the lens of covenant violation.

Stuart argues that 'prepare to meet your God' draws on the theophany tradition of Sinai (Exod 19:11, 15). Israel must prepare not for worship but for confrontation with the living God. The doxology (v. 13) is not a hymn of praise but a declaration of the judge's credentials: the one who forms mountains and reveals thoughts to mortals is fully capable of executing judgment.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman structure the five plagues as a reverse conquest narrative: where God once gave Israel land, food, and victory, he now removes them systematically. The fivefold refrain creates what they call a 'liturgy of futility' — a catalog of failed divine interventions that builds inexorably toward the final theophanic confrontation of v. 12.

hist.context: This passage recounts five escalating divine punishments, each followed by the refrain 'yet you have not returned to me.' The plagues echo the Egyptian plagues and the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28: famine, drought, crop blight, pestilence, and military overthrow. Each was a warning intended to provoke repentance, and each failed. The fivefold refusal forms one of the most poignant passages in prophetic literature — God's grief at his people's stubbornness. The chapter climaxes with a doxology (v. 13) that celebrates God's cosmic power: the one Israel must face is not a local deity but the maker of mountains and wind.

themes.note: Sovereignty at 9: God controls famine, drought, blight, plague, and overthrow. Judgment at 9: escalating punishments build toward final confrontation. Worship at 7: the sarcastic critique of Bethel and Gilgal is central.

rows.label: vv.1-5
rows.text: Cows of Bashan and Sarcastic Worship

rows.label: vv.6-13
rows.text: Five Plagues, Five Refusals, One Doxology

lit.note: Chapter 4 is the second 'Hear this word' speech. It moves from social indictment (cows of Bashan), through religious critique (empty worship), to a devastating plague recital with fivefold refrain, climaxing in a doxology of cosmic judgment.

trans.title: Comparing translations in "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"

translations.text: “I also withheld rain from you when the harvest was still three months away. I sent rain on one town, but withheld it from another. One field had rain; another had none and dried up.

“I also withheld the rain from you when there were yet three months to the harvest; I would send rain on one city, and send no rain on another city; one field would have rain, and the field on which it did not rain would wither;
“Go to Bethel and sin; go to Gilgal and sin yet more. Bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three years.
“Come to Bethel, and transgress; to Gilgal, and multiply transgression; bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three days;

thread.anchor: "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"
thread.text: Isaiah's parallel condemnation of worship without justice: 'I have no pleasure in the blood of bulls and lambs.'

thread.anchor: "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"
thread.text: Amos 4:1 refers to 'cows of Bashan' as a metaphor for wealthy, oppressive women, while Psalm 22:12 uses 'bulls of Bashan' in a context of enemies surrounding the psalmist. The connection is weak and the contexts differ significantly.

thread.anchor: "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"

thread.anchor: "Yet You Have Not Returned to Me"
thread.text: The Revelation parallel: even after plagues, the survivors 'did not repent' — the same pattern of hardened impenitence.

coaching.tip: 'Hear this word, you cows of Bashan' (v. 1). Amos addresses the wealthy women of Samaria as pampered cattle — fat, comfortable, demanding wine while the poor are crushed. The image is deliberately offensive. Prophets use shock to penetrate complacency. Comfortable religion needs uncomfortable language.

'Yet you did not return to me' (vv. 6, 8, 9, 10, 11) — the refrain repeats after each disaster: famine, drought, blight, plague, overthrow. God sent graduated warnings; each was ignored. The catalog of calamities shows divine patience exhausted. Judgment isn't the first resort but the last. Israel had multiple exit ramps.

prayer_prompt: As God sends plague after plague and says 'yet you have not returned to me,' consider asking God whether repeated hardships in your life are random — or whether He is trying to get your attention.
